claims.claim_text: OpenGitOps defines GitOps around declarative desired state, versioned and immutable storage, automatic pull, and continuous reconciliation.
claims.span_hint: section: 1. GitOps Is an Operating Model, Not a Folder of YAML; also Did You Know #1
claims.rationale: The named OpenGitOps principles are directly documented at OpenGitOps, but that host is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: A HorizontalPodAutoscaler can legitimately change a workload's replica count, so not every replica difference is the same kind of drift as a manual image edit.
claims.span_hint: section: 2. Desired State, Actual State, and Drift
claims.rationale: Kubernetes documents that HorizontalPodAutoscaler automatically updates workload resources to scale capacity.

claims.claim_text: Deployment status fields such as available replicas, observed generation, and conditions are runtime observations written by Kubernetes rather than fields usually declared in Git.
claims.span_hint: section: 2. Desired State, Actual State, and Drift; paragraphs after the Deployment YAML
claims.rationale: The Deployment docs show these status fields on live objects, supporting the spec-versus-status distinction.

claims.claim_text: A GitOps controller can mark an application out of sync and either expose the drift or sync it back automatically or manually depending on policy.
claims.span_hint: section: 1. GitOps Is an Operating Model, Not a Folder of YAML; also Did You Know #2
claims.rationale: Argo CD explicitly documents OutOfSync detection plus automatic or manual sync, and Flux documents reconciliation that can be suspended.

claims.claim_text: Pull-based GitOps is safer because it avoids giving an external CI runner broad inbound access to the cluster.
claims.span_hint: section: 4. Pull-Based Reconciliation and Security Boundaries; first paragraph
claims.anchor_text: This pattern is popular because it avoids giving an external CI runner broad inbound access to the cluster.
claims.suggested_rewrite: This pattern is popular because a controller inside the trusted environment pulls reviewed desired state and applies it with its own Kubernetes permissions.
claims.rationale: The pull-based architecture is sourceable, but the comparative safety claim about avoiding external CI access is not directly stated by an allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: Helm is the Kubernetes packaging and rendering layer, not by itself a continuously running GitOps reconciliation controller.
claims.span_hint: section: 5. Tool Responsibilities: Helm, Kustomize, Argo CD, and Flux
claims.rationale: Helm documents itself as the package manager for Kubernetes, while Argo CD documents the controller layer that performs ongoing reconciliation.

claims.claim_text: Kustomize customizes raw, template-free Kubernetes YAML and commonly uses bases, overlays, and patches.
claims.span_hint: section: 5. Tool Responsibilities: Helm, Kustomize, Argo CD, and Flux
claims.rationale: The upstream Kustomize README explicitly describes template-free YAML customization and shows overlays and patches.

claims.claim_text: Argo CD and Flux occupy the reconciliation/controller layer and can reconcile desired state generated from Helm or Kustomize into Kubernetes.
claims.span_hint: section: 5. Tool Responsibilities: Helm, Kustomize, Argo CD, and Flux
claims.rationale: Argo CD documents live-versus-desired comparison and syncing, while Flux documents dedicated Kustomize and Helm controllers that reconcile cluster state.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes admission controllers can reject create or modify requests, so a manifest can render correctly but still fail at apply time because of policy.
claims.span_hint: section: 6. Worked Example: Diagnosing a GitOps Practice Scenario
claims.rationale: The admission controller docs explicitly state that these controllers check modifying requests and can reject them.

further_reading.title: Argo CD Overview
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful for understanding GitOps reconciliation, OutOfSync status, and manual versus automatic sync.

further_reading.title: Flux Core Concepts
further_reading.why_relevant: Provides a concise definition of GitOps and shows how Flux models sources and reconciliation.

further_reading.title: Using RBAC Authorization
further_reading.why_relevant: Directly relevant to the module's discussion of scoped controller permissions and production access boundaries.

notes: Descriptive source_ids are local labels because no shared citation pool ref was provided. Key labels used above: argocd_overview=https://argo-cd.readthedocs.io/en/stable/; flux_concepts=https://fluxcd.io/flux/concepts/; flux_kustomize_controller=https://fluxcd.io/flux/components/kustomize/; flux_helm_controller=https://fluxcd.io/flux/components/helm/; helm_docs=https://helm.sh/docs/; kustomize_readme=https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/kustomize; k8s_deployment_docs=https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/deployment/; k8s_hpa_docs=https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/autoscaling/horizontal-pod-autoscale/; k8s_admission_docs=https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/access-authn-authz/admission-controllers/; opengitops_home=https://opengitops.dev/.
